title: Paget's disease of the nipple - NHS
text:
There are no known causes that lead to a specific type of breast cancer.
In terms of risk factors, you're more likely to develop breast cancer if you:
are older – the risk of developing breast cancer increases with age have close relatives with breast cancer have had breast cancer before have had a type of benign (non-cancerous) breast disease before – certain types of benign breast disease may be associated with an increased risk of breast cancer, but this is only seen in a very small number of women are overweight or living with obesity, particularly if you've been through the menopause (you can use the BMI calculator to check if you're a healthy weight) drink a lot of alcohol – your risk can increase with the  amount of alcohol you drink Read more about the risk factors for breast cancer .
Diagnosing Paget's disease of the nipple You should see your GP if you notice any changes in the skin of your nipple or areola.
As Paget's disease of the nipple is associated with breast cancer, the sooner it's diagnosed, the better the outcome is likely to be.
Also see your GP if you develop a lump in your breast. Although most breast lumps are not cancerous, it's important to have them checked out.
A  biopsy is used to confirm a suspected diagnosis of Paget's disease of the nipple. A small tissue sample (punch biopsy of the skin) will be taken from your nipple and examined under a micro
